summary: Joel compares coverage of Delta Air Lines' new technology system by two IT publications: InformationWeek and Baseline magazine. He finds InformationWeek's article to be a shallow rewrite of press releases, while Baseline's coverage is detailed and investigative. Notably, Baseline reported a two-hour system crash during a blizzard that InformationWeek ignored entirely. Joel holds up Baseline as an example of what industry press should aspire to and recommends readers get a free subscription.
key_points: InformationWeek's coverage of Delta's new IT system was essentially rewritten press releases with vague generalizations
Baseline magazine provided detailed, investigative journalism that included reporting on a system outage InformationWeek omitted
The IT industry press has historically failed to do real journalism, instead republishing vendor-friendly narratives
Joel personally experienced the Delta system crash, adding credibility to his critique of InformationWeek's omission
money_quotes: The difference: while the InformationWeek article is just a poor rewrite of bland warmed-over press releases and vague generalizations, the Baseline article is detailed, interesting, and specific.
In fact the whole Baseline article looks like it was written by an investigative journalist.
It's about time the industry press got its act together.
key_insight: The IT trade press too often serves as a press release republishing service rather than doing real investigative journalism, and publications willing to report inconvenient truths deserve support.
